title: Philippines Building Code Evidence Auditor Source Landscape
purpose: Identify what public data can and cannot support an ECC audit-only workflow for Philippine building-code evidence.
global_safety_rule: The workflow may collect public evidence and identify gaps, but it must not certify legal compliance, structural safety, earthquake safety, or fitness for occupancy.

agent_lanes.goal: Find the legal and professional standards context for building permits, occupancy, structural design, and safety requirements.
what_is_available: DPWH publishes National Building Code / PD 1096 references and PDFs.
DPWH publishes National Building Code Development Office memoranda and fee references.
ASEP publicly describes its role and publications, including National Structural Code volumes and referral-code context.
what_is_hard: The latest full NSCP text may not be openly available as a clean official web document.
A standards agent can cite applicable standards context, but cannot determine whether a specific building design complies without plans and professional review.
agent_lanes.workflow_use: Use as standards context and citation baseline, not as direct proof of a building's compliance.

agent_lanes.goal: Map LGU Office of the Building Official sources for building permits, occupancy permits, annual inspections, certificates, and complaints.
what_is_available: Several LGUs publish OBO/DBO pages explaining services and permit authority.
Some LGUs publish citizen charters, checklists, downloadable forms, and online application references.
Davao City publicly lists building permit, occupancy permit, annual inspection, certification, and complaints on National Building Code violations as OCBO functions.
what_is_hard: Building-specific permit and occupancy records are not consistently searchable online across LGUs.
Many OBO workflows are application or request workflows rather than public databases.
A not-found result does not imply that no permit exists.

sources.name: Quezon City Department of the Building Official
Davao City Office of the City Building Official
Taguig local building office citizen charter PDF

agent_lanes.workflow_use: Use to identify city-specific request paths, required documents, and evidence categories for the packet.

agent_lanes.goal: Normalize the exact building or establishment name, address, use type, operator, and jurisdiction before searching compliance evidence.
what_is_available: LGU business permit pages can help identify business-permit workflows and responsible offices.
SEC services can support company/operator lookup, though some documents require paid or manual access.
Public web, official pages, maps, news, and establishment websites can help identify the correct building and operator.
what_is_hard: Building names are ambiguous and may refer to tenants, malls, towers, hotels, or corporate entities.
Ownership, operator, developer, and contractor may be different entities.
Public identity evidence should be treated as confidence-building, not final proof.

sources.name: Quezon City Business Permits and Licensing Department
Taguig Business Permits and Licensing Office

agent_lanes.workflow_use: Use as a prerequisite lane before any compliance evidence search; stop or ask for clarification when building identity confidence is low.

agent_lanes.goal: Check public traces for contractors, architects, engineers, developers, and professional/license verification routes.
what_is_available: PCAB has public contractor license verification for regular, special, pakyaw, exemption, and suspended/revoked records.
PRC has public professional license verification, but searches often require exact names, license numbers, or other details.
SEC and public developer pages can help identify companies tied to a building.
what_is_hard: Public building pages often do not name the contractor, architect, engineer, or engineer of record.
Professional verification may require exact identifiers and does not prove involvement in a specific building.
The agent should verify a named party when found; it should not invent missing parties.

sources.name: CIAP listing for PCAB online license verification

agent_lanes.workflow_use: Use only after a contractor or professional name is identified from source evidence.

agent_lanes.goal: Find public evidence of earthquake damage, red/yellow tags, closure orders, inspections, repairs, retrofits, building collapse, or safety incidents.
what_is_available: News and LGU pages report post-earthquake inspections, red/yellow tags, closures, reopening decisions, and damaged structures.
Davao City publicly reported red-tagged and yellow-tagged establishments after rapid visual inspection by OCBO and PICE Davao City Chapter.
DPWH and news sources provide examples of advice to seek structural engineer inspection before reopening after earthquakes.
what_is_hard: News evidence may be incomplete, stale, or not tied to official permit records.
Incident history can support follow-up risk flags, but it cannot prove current structural condition.
Social media evidence should be lower confidence unless confirmed by LGU, official agency, or reputable news.

sources.name: Supreme Court E-Library PD 1096 dangerous buildings provisions

agent_lanes.workflow_use: Use as high-value public evidence for safety follow-up and manual engineering review flags.

agent_lanes.goal: Identify records that may exist but require FOI, LGU, OBO, BFP, seller, owner, admin, or professional request workflows.
what_is_available: FOI pages show building permit, FSIC, and building-year/construction-type requests as real request paths.
Some FOI requests succeed, some are denied, and many require clarification or agency-specific routing.
BFP and OBO certificate records may be case-specific and not broadly public.
what_is_hard: Certified true copies, FSICs, detailed plans, as-built plans, structural calculations, and occupancy permits may not be obtainable through simple public web search.
Many records may involve personal data, fees, physical requests, or owner/admin cooperation.
The agent should produce a manual follow-up checklist, not pretend unavailable records were checked.

sources.name: FOI certified true copy of building permit request
FOI BFP denied request listing with FSIC examples
FOI SBMA building year and construction type request
FOI AIC Burgundy Empire Tower FSIC request

agent_lanes.workflow_use: Use to generate a manual follow-up checklist and to label records as not publicly verifiable when appropriate.

top_level_findings.finding: The workflow is feasible as an evidence auditor, not as a compliance certifier.
top_level_findings.basis: Standards, LGU process pages, portals, incident reports, and FOI routes are public enough to build evidence packets, but building-specific compliance proof is often not public.

top_level_findings.finding: The first automation target should be source discovery, identity normalization, citation capture, and confidence labeling.
top_level_findings.basis: The same source category can be public in one city, manual in another, and blocked or absent in a third.

top_level_findings.finding: The strongest public evidence categories are standards context, LGU process pages, PCAB/PRC verification routes, and incident/closure reports.
top_level_findings.basis: These were discoverable through official agency, LGU, FOI, and reputable news/government-news sources.

top_level_findings.finding: The weakest categories are actual building plans, structural calculations, certified permits, and current safety condition.
top_level_findings.basis: These generally require OBO/BFP/FOI/owner/admin/professional workflows and cannot be inferred from absence of web evidence.
